sentences.text: Well, I can only recall Jewish life before the war as seen through an eye of-- eyes of a child.
We-- I practically-- we just slept in our house.
And we had a business there.
But we spent our life on the other side of the border, since our-- all-- entire family, except for my two grandmothers, was-- lived on the other side.
As I said before, this was all Germany at one time.
My aunt lived in Berlin.
And what I recall from the life of-- of our family, of our cousins and uncles and aunts, there were opportunities for German Jews in Germany, like there were for any other German.
There was no difference.
German Jews were members of the Berlin-- Berlin Philharmonics.
They would be artists, actors, doctors, professors, scientists, judges.
Before the Nazis, every door was open to Jewish people.
Sure, there must have been anti-Semitism, but if you don't seek it, then it isn't there.
There is anti-Semitism everywhere, but I have never heard of my family-- my-- or my parents talking about any discrimination.
Anything you wanted to be, it was open to you.
You want to go to university-- it wasn't like Poland, where they had numerous clauses where you couldn't attend university.
German Jews wanted to go to the university, then it was there for them.
They signed up, registered, just like any other German citizen.
German Jews fought in World War I.
My father was a decorated soldier, many of my mother's uncles.
We had some relatives that lost their lives during World War I and died for Germany, and who were decorated and were in high positions in government and so on.
Then, in 1932 and so on, you could already hear about there were some unrests, and there were some agitation.
I came face-to-face with it when the Nazis were elected.
And we must always remember that the Nazis were elected.
They didn't come in by any revolution or a coup.
The people voted for the Nazis because, after World War I, Germany was brought to their knees by the Allies.
And-- and it couldn't last.
It was the biggest mistake that was made.
Up come Hitler, and-- and tells the people, we'll get everything back and we'll have work.
And of course they would support this kind of an idea.
People go for this to better their lives and to get the German pride back.
But even then, in 1933, the Jews still had their businesses.
The teachers, professors, were still at the universities.
And the doctors had their privileges in the hospitals.

But once the Nazis came to power, I-- I always heard, whenever we got together with our family, and I always heard those stories like this professor lost his job, and the doctor did not get hospital privileges anymore.
And then-- and it-- slowly, slowly the noose was tightened.
When you couldn't have anymore a maid.
And German Jews were well-to-do, and they had their nannies and their cooks and their chauffeurs and the maids.
And they were all German.
German Jews were quite well-off.
You couldn't have German help anymore.
German-- Jewish actress, actors, couldn't get a part in a play.
Opera singers could not get a-- a part in an opera.
And slowly, you could not go to a park and sit on a bench.
You had 'Juden Verboten.' There were many places, many hotels, where my aunt used to spend their summer-- her summers in the mountains in the different [INAUDIBLE].
Some hotels would no longer accept her reservation.
And these are the stories I always heard being told.
The schools, Jewish students, you were no longer allowed into a German university.
So slowly, the Jews were isolated from mainstream.
As it-- as the years went on, Jews were no longer allowed to have their own businesses.
Every Jewish business got a German manager, but the Jew was still responsible for the success of the business.
And if the business didn't do well, they were punished and fined for the losses that were incurred by the German manager.
In our family, there was one intermarriage.
My father's half brother.
His name was Walter Kaufman, because he was-- my grandmother remarried, and the name was Kaufman.
He married a non-Jewish woman.
My mother never accepted intermarriage, and she would not have.
But she-- it wasn't her son that married a non-Jewish woman.
But there was intermarriage.
And they were very good marriages.
And many non-Jews-- non-Jewish people saved their spouses during the war in Germany.
No, they weren't.
They had Jewish-- they had non-Jewish friends.
They had-- there were Jewish organizations that were connected to the synagogues.
There were youth groups.
And Jews were organized through the synagogues, especially.
Well, in big cities, the Jewish kids had the-- the opportunity to belong to a Jewish organization.
And I was always envious of that.
But then they were forbidden.
You-- during the Nazi time, you could no longer belong to a Jewish organization.
And so the all the-- the privileges of a citizen of a country were slowly taken away from the German Jews, who were citizens of Germany.
Right.
Right.
I couldn't tell you, because I was too young at that time.
So I would not really know about it.
The only things that I know is what was happening within the families and within Jewish life.
And how slowly-- for instance, my father had a school friend.
They went to school together.
They were the same age.
He was German-- so was my father, but he happened to be Jewish.

They were very close friends, and they were visiting each other and spending-- they would come to our holidays to visit with us, and my parents would go for Christmas and visit with them and exchange gifts.
And when 1933 the Nazis came to power, he was an engineer in the coal mine in Hindenburg.
And I remember that-- I was seven years old.
And I remember when he came at night through the back door, knocked quietly on the door, and came in.
And he sat down with my father and said, it's very difficult to tell you what I came to tell you, but I had to join the Nazi party in order to stay-- keep my position in the coal mine.
But the-- the first prerequisite was that I have no Jewish friends.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He says, we will always be friends.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But we can't talk to each other any more.
We can't see each other any more.
That was the first time that I realized that life is not going to be the same.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were so close.
What is interesting, that at the end, they both were victims of the Nazis.
My father, who never came back from consideration camp.
He, who fought on the Russian front, died as prisoner in Russia in the Soviet Union.
Both were victims.
His first name was Emmanuel.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember his last name.
That was--
That was--
--very shortly after the Nazis came to power.
It must have been '33, '34.
But this is what was happening.
This is the beginning of the Nazis in power and the changes that took place slowly and gradually.
Till-- the-- the-- the beginning of the end was Kristallnacht on November 8, 1938.
There was no desecration of graves before that, or no burning of synagogues.
That was all 1938 on Kristallnacht.
The German Jews did not perceive Hitler as a threat.
They thought he was a joke.
I remember overhearing conversation. 1936, I went for the Berlin Olympics.
I stayed was my aunt and uncle.
As a matter of fact, I saw Hitler and Goering when we were in a bus near the Brandenburg Gate.
They came to lay a wreath.
And it was a big celebration, the opening of the Olympic games.
And it was very interesting that that's the first time in my life that I realized that I am not part of the rest of the people.
I was in the bus-- in Berlin they have those buses like in London, double-deckers.

I was sitting on the top-- because for me, coming from a little town, to sit in that bus was a big event-- and looking at Hitler and Goering in that open car as they came, got out of the car, and this whole ceremony.
The bus and all traffic stopped, and everybody Sieg Heil and Sieg Heil, and saluting.
And I wasn't part of these people anymore.
And that I recall was the first time that I realized that I am different.
I was 10 years old.
Not even 10 yet.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Confusion.
Knowing how my mother was loyal to the Kaiser and always hearing their conversation, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] and suddenly, I'm not part of these people.
Why?
That was the first time I realized.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, of course, German Jews, they were athletes, but they were not participating in the Olympics.
There was no-- no way they could participate in the Olympic games.
I did not have to ask, because I was always there when the conversations were.
By that time already, you know, as I told you, many changes have taken place.
For instance, the Berlin Philharmonics, when you get a job as a musician, this is for life till your retirement.
All Jewish musicians were fired.
That's the first time in the history of the Berlin Philharmonics that they would fire a musician.

And all these-- and so what happened then, the Jewish community, because they couldn't attend theater, and because all these actors couldn't get a part in a play, they then had their own Jewish theaters.
And then a Jewish cultural life began to flourish, which something terrible was happening, and it turned out to really awaken the Jewishness in-- in the German Jews.
Before it was only a religious affiliation where the German Jews were religiously Jewish.
When they no longer were be able to find a position or a job, suddenly there was another side to their Jewishness, and they became culturally Jewish.
And I remember still the songs that they-- that my mother would sing when she went to a Jewish cabaret.
See, we're talking now about times before 1939 when the Jews were still walking the streets, and they did not wear the Star of David.
And they were-- you know, you couldn't recognize them in the streets.
And they still had some of-- some of their freedoms.
It wasn't so easy to go to America.
The quotas were very low.
You couldn't take with you any money.
You know, German Jews were not very good in learning languages.
My mother died here in Toronto and she still spoke German only.
So it was very-- a very difficult decision for German Jews to leave Germany.
Yes.
It awoke the Jewish community after the Nazi came to power.
There were youth groups that went what they called on Hachshara.
They were training what they would do in Palestine.
One of my uncles went to Palestine in 1935.
Unfortunately, when he got there, he couldn't get a job, because he didn't speak Hebrew or Arabic or English.
And so he came back.
But this is when German Jews were starting to look to-- towards Palestine, especially young people.
Dachau, Buchenwald, these camps were not set up for Jews.
Those camps were set up for Communists, for homosexuals, for criminals, or for whatever offense you committed.
It began with-- much later that Jews really were being harassed where they would be called-- oh, I'll never forget when a relative would be called to come to the Gestapo headquarters.
How the whole family was in fear.
Why are they calling him?
They just called them for interview, just to harass them.
What-- what was difficult living in Germany was that you had the Brownshirts, who really were the SR, which were on the loose.
And they were out.
Whenever they, you know, saw Jews in the street, they would harass them.
But it was, at that time not, yet state-orchestrated.
We would always look at them as a bunch of hoodlums rather than anything that was state-orchestrated.
And maybe the German Jews lived in a land of-- of-- of dreams, you know, and didn't realize that what they thought was a work of-- or action of hoodlums, that it actually was state-orchestrated.
But we are German Jews.
We fought for this country.
We are decorated as German soldiers.
We have made contribution to this Country.
We're German citizens.
This is impossible.
And by the way, Hitler is not going to last too long.
That's-- that's all I remember of conversations in-- when we visited our relatives.
As I said before, my-- I went for my religious education and lessons to-- across the border to Hindenburg.
And sometimes when the weather wasn't that great, my father would take me and visit relatives while I had my lesson and take me back.
And I'll never forget the day-- night of November.
My father took me, and we come close to the synagogue, and I smelled smoke.
And I thought there is somewhere a house on fire.
And as we came to the synagogue, we realized that the synagogue was burning.
And it was a rainy day.
A typical November day where the leaves were already-- had fallen on the street.

And there in front of the synagogue were Torah scrolls still smoldering, prayer books, beautifully-bound in leather, as you could only see in the German synagogues, all out there in the streets smoldering, mostly burned, half still burning.
And the SR marching around and not letting anybody near.
People looking.
And of course, as we turned around-- didn't know what happened-- and turned around and went to visit our relatives.
And whenever we came, the doors were locked.
And we-- my father couldn't understand why nobody's home, till at one-- one family.
We kind of sat in the motorcycle and looked up into the windows, and they noticed us.
There were-- the curtains were drawn, and they opened the door just to let us in.
And that's when we found out what happened during the night.
I remember their-- their apartment was totally demolished.
All the beautiful china, crystal, broken.
And then we heard the horrific stories what happened during the night, how they came and broke everything and took the men and dragged them out, marched them through the town.
And that's when we found out the synagogue was burned by the Nazis.
It was Kristallnacht, and that's how I found out about Kristallnacht.
The-- the people did not concern themselves.
You see, this is why it was possible to do what they did.
The neighbors never asked what happened.
The neighbors never came and say, can I help in any way?
Or, do you need anything?
It wasn't their business.
The churches, the priests, no one spoke up on behalf of the Jews.
There was a silence in the land.
Suddenly the Jews were no longer their responsibility.
They might have been their friends at one time, but now they were told that they cannot associate with them anymore, and so the Jews were isolated.
And the propaganda of Goebbels and Streicher with his which, by the way, I have a copy of here.

And the propaganda and the lies and the way the Jews were portrayed as the enemies of the people, of the German people, and how they were blamed for all the awful things and for the loss of the war and what the Allies did to Germany.
They incited them to become anti-Semites.
Children-- now, remember, children joined the Nazi youth organization in 1933.
If a child was 15, in 1938, he was 18.
And if he was brainwashed to hate the Jews, and so many reasons were given to him, it wasn't difficult to become an anti-Semite and hate the Jews.
You blame them for every-- all the ills of the country and of the people.
Oh, definitely.
And it was even before, because there was unemployment in the-- the-- the mines did not work for a time.
And it was all a ploy.
You see, most of the mines in Upper Silesia were owned by Germans, because this was Germany.
And it was in Hitler's interest to create unemployment across the border.
So the German owners slowly closed the mines for no reason at all other than having those people unemployed and create unrest.
And now your counterparts on the other side of the border work day and night.
There is no unemployment under the Nazis.
So you can see the political implications here.
Consequently-- the people that were real friends would come at night through the back door when no one saw them.
The rest of them would not come.
They would go to a non-Jewish store.
So my parents felt it, yes, very much in the business.
Because if they wanted to keep the job in the German coal mine, they could not buy merchandise from us.
So this was the only change.
Otherwise, we did not feel other changes.
The people still were the same as they were before, just a fear that they would lose the job.
And of course, they have to make a living to support their families.
In Germany, the children were brainwashed in schools to tell, in school, everything that goes on in their homes.
So the children spied on their parents.

And very often, it resulted that parents were taken to Dachau or Buchenwald for subversion, because the children would overhear something or would know that their parents still associate, secretly, with Jewish friends.
They would tell the teacher in school.
Well, first of all, after the Kristallnacht, when they took all the Jewish-- not all, but thousands and thousands of Jewish males.
And that's when they put them to camps like Buchenwald and Dachau.
The only way to get out of Dachau or Buchenwald was if you had a visa to emigrate.
And so it was mad rush on all the consulates-- South America, South Africa, United States, Canada.
And we, of course, know the track record of these countries that had no room for Jews.
Children were being sent out to England.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews desperately tried to get out, but there was no way.
My relatives got on that boat, which was turned away from Cuba and came back to Germany.
There was really no way for the German Jews to leave.
